answer:
Yes — there is relevant research, though not usually framed exactly as “interesting Google research job vs. higher-paid OpenAI leadership job.” The closest literatures are job-design, intrinsic/extrinsic motivation, person-job fit, pay and well-being, and regret/affective forecasting.

## Short version

Across the literature, **intrinsic job factors** — interesting tasks, autonomy, use of skills, learning, meaning, fit with one’s abilities/interests, and good colleagues/manager — are usually **stronger predictors of job satisfaction** than pay, especially once pay is already high enough to remove financial stress.

But “leading a small team” is not merely extrinsic. Leadership can satisfy autonomy, competence, impact, mentoring, and status needs. So the real comparison is not:

> interesting work vs. money

but rather:

> technical/research interest + current environment  
> vs. leadership growth + salary + possibly less intellectual engagement + different culture/stress

The evidence would generally say: **do not underestimate day-to-day work content and autonomy**, and **do not expect a modest salary increase to compensate for a large loss in intrinsic interest**. But if the leadership role is deeply motivating to you, that may itself be an intrinsic factor.

---

## 1. Interesting, autonomous work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

A major line of research is the **Job Characteristics Model** from Hackman & Oldham. It argues that jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they provide:

- skill variety  
- task identity  
- task significance  
- autonomy  
- feedback  
- opportunities for growth and responsibility

Meta-analyses broadly support this.

A large meta-analysis by **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007** reviewed hundreds of studies on work design. They found that motivational job characteristics — especially **autonomy, task significance, skill variety, feedback, and complexity** — were strongly related to job satisfaction, internal motivation, and performance.

Typical correlations between enriched job characteristics and job satisfaction are often in the **r ≈ .3 to .5** range, which is fairly large in organizational psychology.

So when people say “I like the actual work,” that is not a trivial preference. It is one of the better-supported predictors of satisfaction.

Relevant papers:

- Hackman & Oldham, 1976, “Motivation through the design of work”
- Fried & Ferris, 1987, meta-analysis of the Job Characteristics Model
- Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007, meta-analysis of work design

---

## 2. Pay matters, but its relationship with job satisfaction is surprisingly modest

A useful meta-analysis is **Judge et al., 2010**, “The relationship between pay and job satisfaction.” They found that **actual pay level** was only weakly related to job satisfaction — around **r ≈ .15**.

That does not mean money is irrelevant. It matters for security, options, fairness, family needs, housing, freedom, etc. But once you are already well compensated, the marginal effect of “somewhat more salary” on day-to-day happiness is often much smaller than people expect.

The income/well-being literature says something similar but with nuance:

- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010** found that emotional well-being rose with income up to a point, while life evaluation kept rising.
- **Killingsworth, 2021**, and **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023** found less evidence of a hard plateau: experienced well-being continues to rise with log income for many people, including at high incomes.
- But the relationship is roughly logarithmic: going from $50k to $100k matters much more than going from $500k to $550k, psychologically speaking.

For someone already in a high-paying research engineering role, a “bit higher salary” would usually be expected to have a **small average effect** unless it changes your life constraints meaningfully — e.g. location, retirement horizon, family security, ability to fund independent work, etc.

Relevant papers:

- Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010
- Kahneman & Deaton, 2010
- Killingsworth, 2021
- Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023

---

## 3. Person-job fit and interest alignment are strongly related to satisfaction

Another relevant literature is **person-job fit** and **vocational interest congruence**.

A meta-analysis by **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005** found that person-job fit is strongly associated with job satisfaction, organizational commitment, and lower intent to quit. The job-satisfaction correlation for person-job fit is often reported around **r ≈ .5+**.

Vocational interest congruence — doing work that matches your interests — also predicts satisfaction and persistence, though the effects are usually more modest than broad person-job fit. Meta-analyses by researchers such as **Assouline & Meir** and **Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow** find positive associations between interest fit and satisfaction/performance.

Interpretation: people tend to be happier and stay longer when the work fits their interests and abilities. “This work is interesting to me” is a real predictor, not just a luxury preference.

Relevant papers:

- Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005, person-environment fit meta-analysis
- Assouline & Meir, 1987, vocational interest congruence meta-analysis
- Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow, 2012, vocational interests meta-analysis

---

## 4. Meaningful work is also highly correlated with job and life satisfaction

“Interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical, but they often overlap in research careers. A meta-analysis by **Allan et al., 2019** found that meaningful work is strongly associated with:

- job satisfaction  
- engagement  
- life satisfaction  
- lower withdrawal intentions

Some reported correlations between meaningful work and job satisfaction are quite high, often around **r ≈ .5 to .6**, though much of this literature is correlational.

This matters because a technically interesting research role may provide meaning through mastery, discovery, autonomy, and contribution. But a leadership role may also provide meaning through mentoring, leverage, organizational impact, and team-building.

Relevant paper:

- Allan et al., 2019, “Outcomes of meaningful work: A meta-analysis”

---

## 5. Autonomy and competence are central in self-determination theory

Self-determination theory, associated with **Deci & Ryan**, argues that well-being and motivation depend heavily on satisfying three psychological needs:

1. autonomy  
2. competence  
3. relatedness

At work, satisfaction of these needs predicts engagement, well-being, and performance. Meta-analytic work by **Van den Broeck et al., 2016** supports this.

This is relevant because the two options may satisfy these needs differently:

| Need | Current interesting research role | Proposed leadership role |
|---|---|---|
| Autonomy | Maybe high if you shape research direction | Maybe higher or lower depending on org/team constraints |
| Competence | Strong technical mastery | New competence domain: people leadership |
| Relatedness | Depends on current team | Could increase through mentoring/team-building |
| Meaning | Research discovery/technical contribution | Impact through others |
| Interest | High | You expect lower |

So the leadership role might still be psychologically rewarding if it gives you autonomy, competence growth, and relatedness. But if it mostly means coordination, meetings, pressure, and less interesting technical work, the literature would predict risk to satisfaction.

Relevant papers:

- Deci & Ryan, 2000
- Van den Broeck et al., 2016, meta-analysis on basic psychological needs at work

---

## 6. Promotions and management roles have mixed evidence

The research on promotions and managerial transitions is not uniformly positive.

Promotions often increase pay, status, autonomy, and perceived success. Some studies find positive associations with job satisfaction. But promotions also bring more stress, responsibility, politics, meetings, conflict management, and time pressure.

One interesting paper by **Boyce & Oswald, 2012** looked at promotions and health in British panel data. They found that promotion did not clearly improve physical health and was associated with worse mental health, plausibly due to increased stress. That does not mean “don’t get promoted,” but it suggests that status advancement is not automatically happiness-enhancing.

A leadership role is beneficial when it gives real autonomy, influence, growth, and meaningful relationships. It can be harmful when it replaces intrinsically valued work with administrative burden and stress.

Relevant papers:

- Boyce & Oswald, 2012, “Do people become healthier after being promoted?”
- Kosteas, 2011, on promotion and job satisfaction
- Karasek, 1979, demand-control model of job stress

---

## 7. People often overweigh salient extrinsic factors and underweigh daily experience

There is a broader judgment/decision-making literature showing that people can mispredict what will make them happy.

Examples:

- **Kahneman’s “focusing illusion”**: when thinking about one factor, such as income or prestige, people overestimate its importance to overall well-being.
- **Hsee & Hastie, 2006**, and related work on decision vs. experience, argue that people often choose options that look better on objective or socially legible dimensions, even when those options do not maximize experienced happiness.
- The commuting literature, e.g. **Stutzer & Frey, 2008**, shows that people often accept longer commutes for higher pay but are not fully compensated in well-being. The general lesson is that recurring daily experiences can matter more than people forecast.

This is relevant because salary and title are salient at decision time. But the actual work content, meetings, autonomy, colleagues, and stress are experienced every day.

Relevant papers:

- Kahneman et al. on focusing illusion
- Hsee & Hastie, 2006, “Decision and experience: why don’t we choose what makes us happy?”
- Stutzer & Frey, 2008, “Stress that doesn’t pay: The commuting paradox”

---

## 8. Direct evidence on regret is weaker

There is less direct research on whether people later regret choosing interesting work over higher pay.

General regret research finds that major regrets often involve:

- education  
- career  
- romance/family  
- missed opportunities  
- failures to act on one’s “ideal self”

For example, **Roese & Summerville, 2005** found that education and career are among the most common domains of regret. **Gilovich & Medvec, 1995** found that over the long run, people tend to regret inactions more than actions. **Davidai & Gilovich, 2018** found that people’s most enduring regrets often involve failing to live up to their ideal self rather than their obligations.

But this does not cleanly answer your case. You could regret either:

- leaving unusually interesting research too early, or
- not taking the chance to lead a team when you had the opportunity.

The regret literature suggests asking: “Which choice would feel more like failing to pursue an important possible self?” For you, that could be “serious researcher” or “technical leader/manager.” The answer is individual.

Relevant papers:

- Gilovich & Medvec, 1995
- Roese & Summerville, 2005
- Davidai & Gilovich, 2018

---

## Practical evidence-based takeaway

Based on the research, I would summarize the base rates like this:

1. **Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work is highly predictive of job satisfaction.**
2. **Pay is predictive too, but weakly once you are already well paid.**
3. **Leadership can be deeply satisfying, but only if it provides autonomy, growth, impact, and relatedness — not just status and meetings.**
4. **People often adapt to salary/title increases faster than to daily work content, stress, commute, autonomy, and relationships.**
5. **Regret evidence is ambiguous; people regret missed career opportunities, but the relevant “missed opportunity” depends on your identity and long-term goals.**

So the research does not say “always stay in the more interesting job.” But it does suggest being cautious about trading a highly interesting role for a moderately higher salary unless the new role offers other strong intrinsic rewards — leadership growth, autonomy, meaningful impact, great colleagues, or a path toward work you will later find interesting.

answer:
Yes — there is a fairly large research base, though not usually phrased as “interesting work vs. slightly higher pay.” The closest literatures are job design, intrinsic motivation, person–job fit, income/well-being, and regret.

## Short version

Research generally finds that, once pay is “good enough” and not perceived as unfair, **the nature of the work itself** — autonomy, challenge, skill use, learning, meaning, fit with interests, and good colleagues/managers — is more strongly related to job satisfaction and engagement than additional pay.  

So if “much less interesting” really means less autonomy, less learning, less intellectual challenge, less meaning, or worse fit, the evidence would predict lower job satisfaction on average. But if the OpenAI role gives you a long-desired leadership path, autonomy, impact, and growth, that may itself count as intrinsically motivating work rather than merely an extrinsic perk.

## 1. Pay matters, but extra pay has relatively weak links to job satisfaction

A well-known meta-analysis:

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010 — “The relationship between pay and job satisfaction”**  
They found that actual pay level has only a **small correlation** with job satisfaction, often around **r ≈ .15** depending on measure. In plain English: people who are paid more are somewhat more satisfied, but the relationship is weak.

This does not mean pay is irrelevant. Pay matters a lot when it affects security, fairness, debt, family constraints, or status concerns. But at already-high compensation levels, a “bit higher salary” is unlikely to dominate day-to-day work experience.

Related subjective well-being research:

- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010**: emotional well-being rose with income up to a point in their data, with diminishing returns.
- **Killingsworth, 2021**: found experienced well-being continues rising with income, roughly log-linearly.
- **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023**: reconciled these results; for many people well-being keeps increasing with income, but for the least happy subgroup it plateaus around moderately high incomes.

The common implication: **more money helps, but the marginal benefit of extra income is usually smaller when you are already financially comfortable.**

## 2. “Interesting work” maps onto some of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

The job-design literature is very relevant here.

Classic model:

**Hackman & Oldham, 1976 — Job Characteristics Model**  
They argued that jobs are motivating when they have:

- skill variety,
- task identity,
- task significance,
- autonomy,
- feedback.

Later meta-analyses supported much of this.

**Fried & Ferris, 1987** and **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007** meta-analyses found that motivational job characteristics — especially autonomy, task significance, feedback, complexity, and skill variety — are meaningfully associated with job satisfaction, intrinsic motivation, performance, and lower burnout.

Typical correlations between these job features and job satisfaction are often in the **medium range**, e.g. roughly **r ≈ .30–.50**, depending on the characteristic and study. That is generally stronger than the pay-level/job-satisfaction association.

So, if your current Google role is “very interesting” because it gives you autonomy, deep technical challenge, learning, feedback, and meaning, the literature would treat that as a major positive, not a luxury.

## 3. Interest fit predicts satisfaction and persistence, though not perfectly

There is also a person–environment fit / vocational interest literature.

Relevant meta-analyses include:

- **Assouline & Meir, 1987**
- **Tranberg, Slane & Ekeberg, 1993**
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005**
- **Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow, 2012**

The broad result: when people’s interests and values fit their work, they tend to have higher job satisfaction, more commitment, better persistence, and sometimes better performance. The effects are usually **modest but reliable** — often around **r ≈ .20–.30** for satisfaction-like outcomes.

So yes: people in jobs that better match their interests tend to be happier at work. But fit is not everything. Manager quality, autonomy, workload, team climate, and life constraints also matter.

## 4. Intrinsic motivation and basic psychological needs are strongly linked to well-being

Self-determination theory is another relevant body of evidence.

It emphasizes three psychological needs:

- **autonomy** — feeling you have agency,
- **competence** — feeling effective and growing,
- **relatedness** — feeling connected to others.

At work, satisfaction of these needs is strongly associated with engagement and well-being.

A useful review/meta-analysis:

**Van den Broeck et al., 2016 — basic psychological needs at work**  
They found that autonomy, competence, and relatedness satisfaction are strongly related to job satisfaction, engagement, and lower burnout.

This matters for your case because “leading a small team” could satisfy autonomy, competence, relatedness, and impact needs. So the key question is not simply:

> Interesting research vs. management.

It is more like:

> Which role better satisfies autonomy, competence/growth, relatedness, meaning, and sustainable workload?

If the OpenAI role is less technically interesting but gives you meaningful leadership, real autonomy, and growth, the tradeoff may be less one-sided than “interesting vs. not interesting.”

## 5. Meaningful work is strongly related to job satisfaction

There is also a meaningful-work literature.

**Allan et al., 2019 — meta-analysis on meaningful work**  
Meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, work engagement, organizational commitment, and somewhat with life satisfaction.

This is relevant because “interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical. Some work is intellectually interesting but not meaningful; some leadership work may be less technically novel but highly meaningful because of people development, strategic impact, or mission.

So you may want to separate:

- intellectual interest,
- mission/impact,
- autonomy,
- learning,
- social/team meaning,
- status/compensation.

The research suggests all are relevant, but intrinsic and meaning-related factors are usually more predictive of sustained satisfaction than pay alone.

## 6. Job satisfaction is substantially related to overall life satisfaction

This is not just “work happiness” in isolation.

Meta-analytic work, e.g.:

**Tait, Padgett & Baldwin, 1989**  
**Bowling et al., 2010**

found that job satisfaction and life satisfaction are correlated, often around **r ≈ .30–.40+**, with evidence of reciprocal effects. Happier people are more satisfied with jobs, but better jobs also appear to improve life satisfaction.

Given how much waking time work occupies, daily work content matters.

## 7. Evidence on regret is weaker and less direct

There is less direct research on:

> Do people regret leaving a more interesting job for higher salary or leadership?

But adjacent regret research says a few relevant things.

**Gilovich & Medvec, 1995** found that over the long run people often regret **inactions** more than actions.  
**Roese & Summerville, 2005** found that common life regrets include education, career, and romance — domains with high perceived opportunity.

This cuts both ways in your case:

- You might regret leaving unusually interesting work.
- Or you might regret not taking the leadership opportunity you “always wanted.”

So the regret literature does not give a clean answer. It suggests that regret is often strongest when people feel they failed to pursue an identity-relevant possibility. If “becoming a team lead” is central to your identity, not trying it could be regret-producing. If doing frontier technical work is central to your identity, leaving it could be regret-producing.

## Research-informed way to frame your decision

The evidence would suggest not treating the choice as:

> interesting work vs. salary.

Instead, rate both roles on the predictors that actually show up in the literature:

1. **Autonomy/control**  
   Will you have real agency over direction, methods, priorities?

2. **Competence/growth**  
   Will you keep learning at a high rate? Will the work stretch you?

3. **Intellectual interest**  
   Will your daily tasks be energizing or draining?

4. **Meaning/impact**  
   Do you believe the work matters?

5. **Relatedness/team quality**  
   Will you enjoy and respect the people you work with?

6. **Leadership fit**  
   Do you actually want the daily reality of management: coaching, hiring, conflict resolution, planning, performance conversations, coordination?

7. **Workload and sustainability**  
   Will the role create chronic stress, ambiguity, or loss of deep-work time?

8. **Compensation/security/fairness**  
   Does the pay difference materially change your life, or is it mostly symbolic?

9. **Option value/reversibility**  
   If you try leadership and dislike it, can you return to deep technical work? If you stay, can you get leadership later?

## Bottom line from the research

The best-supported conclusion is:

> People tend to be happier when their work is intrinsically interesting, autonomous, meaningful, and well matched to their abilities and interests. Additional pay has a positive but usually smaller effect once financial security and fairness are satisfied.

So if the OpenAI role is genuinely much less interesting and the salary increase is only modest, the research would not predict salary alone to compensate.  

But if leading a small team is a major intrinsic goal for you — giving you autonomy, growth, relatedness, and impact — then it should be considered part of the “interesting work” side of the ledger, not merely an external perk.

answer:
Yes. The research is fairly consistent on one high-level point:

**Intrinsic work features — interesting/challenging work, autonomy, fit with your abilities/interests, meaningfulness, learning, task variety — are among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and engagement. Pay matters, but absolute pay level is usually a much weaker predictor, especially once financial security is already high.**

That said, “leading a small team” is not merely an extrinsic perk; it can itself increase autonomy, meaning, growth, influence, and identity. So the real comparison is not “interesting work vs money,” but something like:

> **technical/research interest + current autonomy + Google environment**  
> vs.  
> **less interesting domain + more leadership/autonomy/status/pay + new org/team-building opportunity**

Below are relevant findings.

---

## 1. Interesting, autonomous, meaningful work strongly predicts job satisfaction

A large work-design literature, starting with the **Job Characteristics Model** — Hackman & Oldham, 1976/1980 — finds that jobs are more satisfying and motivating when they have:

- skill variety  
- task identity — seeing a whole piece of work through  
- task significance — feeling the work matters  
- autonomy  
- feedback from the work itself  

A major meta-analysis:

**Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007, Journal of Applied Psychology**  
found that motivational work characteristics — autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, complexity, problem solving, etc. — have substantial relationships with job satisfaction, internal motivation, engagement, and lower turnover intentions. Many corrected correlations are in the roughly **.30–.50** range, which is large by organizational-psychology standards.

In plain English: people are reliably happier in jobs where the daily work is absorbing, varied, self-directed, and feels useful.

---

## 2. Person–job fit is one of the strongest predictors of satisfaction

If by “interesting” you mean “this work fits my abilities, values, and curiosity,” the relevant literature is **person–job fit** and **person–environment fit**.

A widely cited meta-analysis:

**Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005, Personnel Psychology**  
found that person–job fit is strongly associated with:

- job satisfaction  
- organizational commitment  
- lower intention to quit  
- somewhat better performance  

The relationship between person–job fit and job satisfaction was around **r ≈ .50+** in their meta-analysis, again a large effect.

A narrower literature on **vocational interest congruence** — e.g. Holland-type “my interests match my occupation” — finds positive but usually more modest effects, often around **r ≈ .15–.25** with satisfaction. But broader fit — values, skills, preferred work style, autonomy needs — is much stronger.

So if your current role is interesting because it fits your research taste and talents unusually well, that is a serious factor, not a luxury.

---

## 3. Meaningful work is also strongly related to satisfaction and life satisfaction

Another relevant construct is **meaningful work**.

**Allan et al., 2019, Journal of Management Studies**, meta-analysis on meaningful work, found that meaningful work is strongly associated with:

- job satisfaction  
- work engagement  
- lower withdrawal intentions  
- life satisfaction, though more moderately  

The job-satisfaction correlations are often around **.50–.60**, while life-satisfaction relationships are more moderate, around **.25–.35**.

This matters because “interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical. A job can be intellectually interesting but not meaningful, or meaningful but technically dull. Ideally you want both; if you have to trade them off, the literature suggests both matter.

---

## 4. Pay matters, but less than people often expect — especially at high income

There is also direct evidence on pay and job satisfaction.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010, Journal of Vocational Behavior**, meta-analysis, found that **pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction**, roughly around **r ≈ .15**. Pay satisfaction matters more than raw pay, and fairness/relative pay can matter a lot, but absolute salary is not usually one of the dominant predictors of whether people like their jobs.

For general happiness, income does predict well-being, but the effect is closer to **log-income** than linear income: each additional dollar matters less as income rises.

Important studies:

- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010, PNAS**: income improves life evaluation; emotional well-being showed a plateau around then-$75k in their data.  
- **Killingsworth, 2021, PNAS**: experienced well-being continued rising with income beyond that level.  
- **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023, PNAS**: reconciliation paper; for most people, well-being continues rising with income, but the least-happy subgroup shows more of a plateau.

The practical interpretation for someone already at Google/OpenAI-level compensation: a **“bit higher salary”** is unlikely to dominate your long-run happiness unless it changes something concrete — financial independence timeline, family security, location, risk tolerance, etc.

---

## 5. Job satisfaction spills over into life satisfaction

Work is not all of life, but job satisfaction and life satisfaction are meaningfully related.

Meta-analyses such as:

- **Tait, Padgett & Baldwin, 1989**
- **Bowling, Eschleman & Wang, 2010**

find correlations between job satisfaction and life satisfaction / subjective well-being often in the **.30–.45** range. The relationship is likely reciprocal: happier people tend to be more satisfied at work, and satisfying work contributes to life satisfaction.

So the “daily experience” of the job matters. If the new role would make 40–60 hours/week substantially less engaging, that is a real cost.

---

## 6. Leadership could be a major positive — if it gives autonomy, growth, and impact

You said leading a small team is something you have always wanted. The research would treat that not as a shallow prestige factor, but potentially as an intrinsic motivator.

Leadership/management can increase:

- autonomy  
- influence  
- task significance  
- social impact  
- identity/status  
- learning and competence  
- ownership of outcomes  

All of those are associated with motivation and satisfaction.

But leadership can also increase:

- meetings  
- coordination burden  
- emotional labor  
- role conflict  
- accountability without full control  
- reduced hands-on research time  

The **Job Demands–Resources** literature — Demerouti et al., Bakker & Demerouti — suggests high demands are less harmful when paired with high resources: autonomy, support, competence, clarity, good team, control over priorities. A leadership role with high responsibility but low strategic autonomy can be draining; a leadership role with real ownership can be deeply satisfying.

So the important empirical question is not just “manager or IC?” but:

> Will this role give you more autonomy, competence growth, relatedness, and meaningful impact — or mostly more coordination overhead?

---

## 7. Regret evidence is weaker, but there are adjacent findings

There is less direct research of the form: “Do people regret leaving more interesting jobs for higher-paying but less interesting ones?”

But adjacent literatures suggest a few things:

### People often overweight salient factors like pay/prestige

Affective forecasting research — e.g. **Kahneman, Schkade, Gilbert, Wilson** — shows that people can overestimate how much salient life changes will affect their happiness. Salary, brand, title, and prestige are very salient at decision time; daily work texture becomes salient only after you start.

This is related to the **focusing illusion**: when you think about one feature, it seems more important than it will feel in lived experience.

### Long-run regret often involves missed growth opportunities

Research by **Gilovich & Medvec, 1995** found that short-term regrets are often about actions taken, but long-term regrets are often about inactions — things people did not try. That is not specific to jobs, but it is relevant if “leading a team” is a long-standing personal goal.

So regret research cuts both ways:

- You might regret leaving unusually interesting work.
- You might also regret never trying the leadership path you wanted.

---

## My research-based synthesis

If you abstract away the company names, the evidence would say:

1. **Do not discount “interesting work.”**  
   It is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction, engagement, and retention.

2. **Do not let a modest salary increase dominate the decision.**  
   At already-high income levels, the marginal well-being gain is likely small unless it changes your financial life materially.

3. **Treat leadership as an intrinsic factor, not just a title.**  
   If leading a small team gives you autonomy, ownership, mentoring, and impact, it may compensate for less technical interest.

4. **The key variable is your expected daily/weekly experience.**  
   How much time will you spend in deep technical/research work vs meetings, planning, people management, product constraints, hiring, alignment, etc.?

5. **The biggest red flag would be low interest + low autonomy.**  
   Less interesting work can be fine if it comes with real ownership and growth. Less interesting work with limited freedom is much more likely to reduce satisfaction.

A useful way to evaluate the offer empirically would be to ask OpenAI for concrete answers:

- What fraction of my time would be hands-on technical/research work?  
- How much agenda-setting authority would I have?  
- Is this a true leadership role or mainly execution management?  
- How strong is the team?  
- What would success look like after 12 months?  
- Can I publish, explore, or shape technical direction?  
- If I dislike management, is there a path back to a strong IC/research role?

Based on the literature, I would give **heavy weight to interestingness, autonomy, fit, and meaningfulness**, and **less weight to a modest salary difference**. But if the OpenAI role genuinely satisfies your desire to lead, gives you real ownership, and opens a career path you value, then “less interesting technical content” may not necessarily mean lower long-term satisfaction.

answer:
There is not much research on the exact choice “more interesting research IC job vs less interesting team-lead job,” but there is a lot on adjacent constructs: **intrinsic work**, **autonomy**, **person–job fit**, **meaningful work**, **pay**, **promotion/management**, and **regret**. The short version is:

> Research generally finds that intrinsically interesting, autonomous, well-fitting work is a strong predictor of job satisfaction and well-being; pay matters, but once basic/comfort needs are met, marginal salary differences are usually a weaker predictor. Leadership/promotion can increase satisfaction if it brings autonomy, competence, impact, and status, but it can also increase stress and reduce time spent on the work one actually enjoys. Evidence on later regret is much weaker and more ambiguous.

Below are the main research findings.

---

## 1. “Interesting work” is usually studied as intrinsic motivation, autonomy, skill variety, task significance, and person–job fit

The classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model**: jobs tend to be more motivating and satisfying when they involve:

- autonomy,
- skill variety,
- task identity,
- task significance,
- feedback,
- meaningfulness.

Meta-analyses generally find that these features have moderate-to-large associations with job satisfaction, internal motivation, and lower burnout.

Relevant sources:

- Hackman & Oldham, 1976, 1980 — Job Characteristics Model.
- Fried & Ferris, 1987 — meta-analysis of job characteristics.
- Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007 — large meta-analysis of work design.

The high-level result: **jobs that give people autonomy, learning, skill use, variety, and meaningful contribution are reliably more satisfying**.

For your case, “very interesting stuff” at Google likely maps onto several of these: intellectual challenge, learning, skill use, maybe autonomy and meaningfulness. If the OpenAI role is “less interesting” because the actual daily work would be more coordination, meetings, execution management, etc., that is not a small detail — day-to-day task content is a major driver of satisfaction.

---

## 2. Person–job fit is one of the stronger predictors of job satisfaction

A very robust finding in organizational psychology is that **fit matters**: people are more satisfied when their job matches their abilities, interests, values, and preferred working style.

A major meta-analysis:

- Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005, *Personnel Psychology*.

They found that **person–job fit** is strongly related to job satisfaction, organizational commitment, and lower intent to quit. The job-satisfaction correlation is often reported around the high .4s to .5s depending on the fit measure — quite large by social-science standards.

So if your current role is unusually aligned with your intellectual interests, that is an important signal. But if leading a small team is also a deep personal interest, then the OpenAI role may have fit along a different dimension: leadership, influence, mentoring, strategy, ownership.

The research would not classify “leading a team” as merely extrinsic. If leadership gives you autonomy, competence, social connection, and meaning, it can be intrinsically rewarding too.

---

## 3. Pay matters, but its marginal effect is usually smaller than people expect — especially at high incomes

There are two relevant bodies of research: job satisfaction and general well-being.

### Pay and job satisfaction

A frequently cited meta-analysis:

- Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010, *Journal of Vocational Behavior*.

They found that **pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction** — roughly a correlation around .15. Pay satisfaction itself is more related to job satisfaction, but absolute pay level is not a huge predictor.

In plain English: people who are paid more are somewhat more satisfied, but salary is usually not among the strongest predictors once you compare it to autonomy, fit, relationships, meaningfulness, and work conditions.

### Income and life satisfaction

For overall well-being:

- Kahneman & Deaton, 2010, *PNAS*: emotional well-being rose with income up to a point, while life evaluation kept rising.
- Killingsworth, 2021, *PNAS*: found experienced well-being continues rising with income, roughly log-linearly.
- Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023, *PNAS*: reconciliation: income continues to help for many people, but the pattern differs across happier vs less-happy groups.

The consensus is not “money stops mattering.” Rather:

> Money matters, but with diminishing marginal returns, and the effect of an incremental raise is usually small compared with big differences in daily life, autonomy, stress, relationships, health, and purpose.

For someone already at Google-level compensation, “a bit higher salary” is probably less important for happiness than whether the new job changes your daily experience, autonomy, learning, stress, or career trajectory.

Exception: if the salary difference materially changes your financial security, family options, housing, immigration/visa risk, ability to support others, or freedom to take future risks, then it may matter more.

---

## 4. Promotions and leadership roles: positive, but not unambiguously

The research on promotions suggests they often raise job satisfaction, at least initially, but can also bring stress.

Relevant studies include:

- Kosteas, 2011 — promotions and promotion expectations are associated with higher job satisfaction.
- Johnston & Lee, 2013 — promotions can bring extra status but also extra stress; effects on overall well-being are not always large or lasting.

This is intuitive: a leadership role can provide:

- autonomy,
- status,
- influence,
- broader impact,
- mentorship,
- career capital,
- growth.

But it can also reduce:

- time doing deep technical work,
- flow,
- individual creativity,
- direct research involvement,
- schedule control.

So the relevant question is not “management good or bad?” but:

> Would the leadership work itself be interesting and identity-congruent for you, or is it mainly a title/status/salary improvement attached to less enjoyable daily work?

If leading a small team is “something I always wanted to do,” that is important. Research on self-determination theory would frame it as a possible source of **competence**, **autonomy**, and **relatedness** — all major psychological needs.

---

## 5. Meaningful work and intrinsic motivation are strongly related to well-being

Self-determination theory — Deci & Ryan and many others — argues that people thrive when work satisfies three basic needs:

- autonomy,
- competence,
- relatedness.

Workplace meta-analyses generally support this: need satisfaction at work is associated with engagement, job satisfaction, and lower burnout.

Relevant sources:

- Deci & Ryan, 2000 — Self-Determination Theory.
- Van den Broeck et al., 2016 — meta-analysis of basic psychological needs at work.
- Allan et al., 2019 — meta-analysis on meaningful work outcomes.

Meaningful work is also strongly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, and life satisfaction. However, much of this literature is correlational: people who are generally happier may also perceive more meaning. Still, longitudinal and intervention evidence broadly supports the idea that autonomy, competence, and meaning are not just “nice extras”; they are central.

---

## 6. Job satisfaction and life satisfaction are meaningfully connected

Meta-analyses find a substantial association between job satisfaction and overall life satisfaction, often around .3–.4 or higher depending on measurement.

Relevant sources:

- Tait, Padgett & Baldwin, 1989 — meta-analysis.
- Bowling, Eschleman & Wang, 2010 — job satisfaction and subjective well-being.

Causality goes both ways: happy people tend to like their jobs more, and satisfying jobs contribute to life satisfaction. But since work consumes a large fraction of waking life, day-to-day job experience matters a lot.

For your situation, this argues against dismissing “interestingness” as a luxury. If the work is genuinely central to your daily engagement and identity, it is likely important.

---

## 7. Regret research: people often regret missed opportunities, but it does not clearly answer your choice

There is research on life regrets, but it is less directly applicable.

Relevant findings:

- Roese & Summerville, 2005: education, career, and romance are among the most common life-regret domains.
- Gilovich & Medvec, 1995: in the short run, people often regret actions; in the long run, they more often regret inactions — things they did not try.

This could cut both ways:

- You might regret not trying leadership when you had a strong opportunity.
- You might regret leaving unusually interesting research for a less intellectually fulfilling role.
- You might regret optimizing for salary/title over daily work.
- Or you might regret staying too long in a comfortable interesting role and not building leadership experience.

The regret literature does **not** say “always take the risk” or “always choose the interesting work.” It mostly says that people regret foregone personally meaningful opportunities, especially when they believe the opportunity was rare.

So the key is: which opportunity would feel more like a rare, identity-relevant missed path?

---

## 8. A useful research-based way to frame your decision

Based on the literature, I would not treat the variables equally. I would roughly separate them like this:

### Strong predictors of satisfaction

- Daily task interest.
- Autonomy.
- Learning and growth.
- Person–job fit.
- Quality of manager/peers.
- Meaning/impact.
- Ability to use your strongest skills.
- Sustainable workload.
- Psychological safety and trust.

### Moderate/context-dependent predictors

- Promotion/title.
- Leadership responsibility.
- Organizational prestige.
- Future optionality.
- Compensation, especially if it changes financial freedom.

### Often over-weighted predictors

- Small salary increases at already-high income.
- Status/title if not accompanied by enjoyable daily work.
- Abstract mission if daily role is misaligned.
- Brand name, unless it creates meaningful optionality.

For your concrete choice, the research would suggest asking:

1. **How much less interesting is “less interesting”?**  
   Is it 10% less intellectually exciting but still meaningful? Or is it a real shift away from what gives you energy?

2. **Is leadership itself interesting to you, or just something you want to have done?**  
   There is a big difference between wanting the identity/status of leading and enjoying the actual work: coaching, delegation, conflict, prioritization, hiring, performance management.

3. **Will the OpenAI role satisfy autonomy/competence/relatedness?**  
   If yes, it may be intrinsically rewarding despite being less technically interesting.

4. **Is the current Google work unusually rare?**  
   If you are doing something you may not be able to find again, that increases the cost of leaving.

5. **Is the leadership opportunity rare?**  
   If you have wanted team leadership for years and this is a credible chance, that increases the cost of staying.

6. **Ignore salary unless it changes your actual life.**  
   Research suggests a modest raise is unlikely to compensate for a large drop in daily work fit.

---

## Bottom line from the research

The evidence most strongly supports this:

> If the OpenAI role is mainly better on salary/title but substantially worse on intrinsic interest and day-to-day fit, the research would predict lower satisfaction risk.  
> If the leadership component is itself deeply motivating and gives you autonomy, growth, impact, and a team you enjoy, then it may compensate for less technical interestingness.

So the research does **not** say “always choose the interesting work.” But it does say you should give **very high weight** to how interesting, autonomous, meaningful, and well-fitting the actual daily work will be — probably more weight than a modest salary increase.

answer:
Yes — there is a lot of relevant research, though it is usually framed as **intrinsic motivation, job design, person–job fit, autonomy, meaning, and pay**, rather than “interestingness” per se.

### Short version

The evidence is fairly consistent:

1. **Interesting, intrinsically motivating work is one of the stronger predictors of job satisfaction and engagement.**
2. **Pay matters, but its marginal effect on happiness/job satisfaction is usually much smaller than people expect, especially once basic financial needs are well covered.**
3. **Leadership/management can increase satisfaction if it brings autonomy, growth, impact, and identity fit — but it can reduce satisfaction if it mainly adds stress, coordination work, and less of the work you actually enjoy.**
4. **People often regret not pursuing personally meaningful growth opportunities, but the regret literature does not imply “always take the higher-status/higher-pay role.”**

So the research would not say “stay at Google” or “go to OpenAI.” But it would say: **do not discount day-to-day work content. It is one of the biggest drivers of work happiness.**

---

## 1. “Interesting work” strongly predicts job satisfaction

The closest research area is the **Job Characteristics Model** from Hackman & Oldham. It argues that jobs are more motivating when they have:

- skill variety  
- task identity  
- task significance  
- autonomy  
- feedback  
- meaningfulness  
- challenge/complexity

These are basically components of “interesting work.”

Meta-analyses generally support this. For example:

- **Fried & Ferris, 1987** meta-analysis: job characteristics such as autonomy, task significance, feedback, and skill variety were meaningfully related to job satisfaction and motivation.
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007** meta-analysis: motivational job features — autonomy, task variety, task significance, complexity, feedback — were among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction, internal motivation, and lower stress.

The effect sizes are usually much larger than the effect of pay on job satisfaction.

A rough summary from this literature:

> People tend to be happier in jobs where the work itself is engaging, challenging, autonomous, and meaningful.

This does not mean every moment must be fascinating. But if the core daily work is boring or misaligned, that tends to matter a lot.

---

## 2. Person–job fit and interest fit matter a lot

Another relevant literature is **person–job fit**: how well your abilities, interests, and values match the job.

A major meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005** found that person–job fit is strongly associated with job satisfaction, organizational commitment, and lower intent to quit.

There is also research from vocational psychology, influenced by Holland’s theory of career interests, showing that **interest congruence** predicts satisfaction and persistence.

The important point is: if you are someone whose happiness depends heavily on intellectual novelty, technical depth, research taste, or scientific curiosity, then a role that reduces those may have a real cost even if it improves salary or title.

---

## 3. Pay matters, but less than people often think

There is a well-known meta-analysis on this:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010**, “The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis.”

They found that actual pay level has only a **weak-to-moderate relationship** with job satisfaction. The correlation between pay and job satisfaction was around **r ≈ .15**, which is much smaller than the association between job satisfaction and autonomy, meaningful work, or person–job fit.

Separately, research on income and subjective well-being finds that money matters, but with diminishing returns.

- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010** found that income was strongly related to life evaluation, but emotional well-being plateaued around a certain income level in their data.
- **Killingsworth, 2021** later found that experienced well-being continues to rise with income, but roughly logarithmically — each additional dollar matters less at higher income levels.
- **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023** reconciled some of this: for many people, happiness continues rising with income, but for less happy people there may be a plateau.

For your case, since you are already in a high-paying research engineering role, a “bit higher salary” is unlikely, on average, to compensate for a large drop in day-to-day interest — unless that extra money changes something major in your life: financial independence, family security, location, runway for future projects, etc.

---

## 4. Autonomy may matter as much as “interestingness”

A job can be “less interesting” in topic but still satisfying if it gives more:

- autonomy  
- ownership  
- influence  
- mastery  
- leadership growth  
- social impact  
- strategic context

Self-Determination Theory — Deci & Ryan; Gagné & Deci — argues that well-being and motivation depend heavily on three needs:

1. **autonomy** — feeling you have agency  
2. **competence** — feeling you are growing and effective  
3. **relatedness** — feeling connected to others

Workplace studies generally find that satisfaction of these needs predicts engagement, satisfaction, and well-being.

So the key distinction is:

> Is the OpenAI role “less interesting” only technically, but more interesting in autonomy/leadership/impact?  
> Or is it less interesting overall in the psychologically important sense?

If leading a small team is something you genuinely want, that could itself be a form of interesting work. But if you mainly want the title or salary, the evidence is less favorable.

---

## 5. Meaningful work predicts well-being

“Interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical, but they often overlap.

A meta-analysis:

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling & Tay, 2019**, on meaningful work, found that meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, life satisfaction, and lower distress.

This suggests that if your current Google work feels deeply meaningful or intellectually alive, that is a major asset. If the OpenAI role is less technically interesting but feels more meaningful or impactful, that could offset some of the loss.

---

## 6. Promotions and management are mixed

Research on promotions and management suggests that promotions often increase:

- pay  
- status  
- perceived achievement  
- job satisfaction, at least initially

But they can also increase:

- stress  
- hours  
- responsibility  
- interpersonal conflict  
- administrative work  
- reduced time doing the craft itself

Some longitudinal work, e.g. **Boyce & Oswald, 2012**, found that promotions do not necessarily improve health and can increase mental strain.

So “leading a small team” is not automatically good or bad. It depends on whether management is part of the work you want to do, not merely a career milestone.

A useful distinction:

- If you want to lead because you enjoy mentoring, setting direction, making people better, and owning outcomes, that aligns with intrinsic motivation.
- If you want to lead mainly because it feels like the next status rung, research would predict weaker long-term happiness benefits.

---

## 7. Regret research: people often regret not trying meaningful paths

The regret literature is relevant but less directly conclusive.

Classic findings:

- **Gilovich & Medvec, 1995**: in the short term, people regret actions more; in the long term, they often regret inactions more.
- **Roese & Summerville, 2005**: among common life regrets, education and career are near the top. People often regret missed opportunities, especially in domains where they feel they had agency.

This suggests that if “leading a team” is a long-standing personal goal, not trying it could become a source of regret.

But this does **not** imply that taking the OpenAI role is automatically better. People can also regret taking a role that looked prestigious but made their daily life worse.

The regret literature mainly says:

> Long-term regret often comes from not pursuing important identity-level goals.

So the question is whether team leadership is truly an identity-level goal for you, or merely an attractive feature of the offer.

---

## 8. People overweigh salient factors like salary/title

Another useful finding comes from affective forecasting and the “focusing illusion.”

- **Kahneman et al., 2006** argued that people often overestimate how much salient life circumstances, such as income, will affect day-to-day happiness.
- **Wilson & Gilbert** work on affective forecasting shows that people often mispredict the duration and intensity of future feelings.

In job choice, salary, company name, and title are very salient. But after the first months, your actual experience may depend more on repeated daily features:

- What problems do I work on?
- Who do I work with?
- How much autonomy do I have?
- Am I learning?
- Do I respect the direction?
- Is my calendar full of things I dislike?
- Do I feel proud of the output?

There is also evidence for “honeymoon-hangover” effects in job changes — satisfaction often rises after changing jobs, then later drifts down as novelty fades. See **Boswell, Boudreau & Tichy, 2005**.

---

## Research-based takeaways for your decision

Based on the literature, I would frame the decision less as:

> interesting work vs. salary/team lead

and more as:

> Which role gives me the better long-term combination of autonomy, growth, meaning, fit, and daily engagement?

A research-informed checklist:

### Current Google role

Ask:

- Is the work interesting in a way that is rare and valuable to you?
- Do you have autonomy?
- Are you still growing?
- Are there paths to leadership internally?
- Would staying feel like continued exploration, or like comfort/inertia?
- Are you underpaid relative to your needs, or merely relative to the offer?

### OpenAI role

Ask:

- Is the “less interesting” work still meaningful?
- Would leading the team be intrinsically rewarding?
- How much of your week would be management, meetings, planning, hiring, performance issues?
- Would you have real authority or just responsibility?
- Is the team/domain likely to become more interesting over time?
- Are you excited by the people and mission enough to compensate for less technical interest?

---

## Bottom line from the research

The strongest evidence says:

> **Day-to-day work content, autonomy, fit, and meaning are major predictors of job satisfaction. Pay has weaker marginal effects, especially at already high income levels.**

But:

> **If leading a team is a deeply held goal and the new role gives you autonomy, growth, and impact, then it may count as “interesting work” in a different dimension.**

So the critical question is not simply whether OpenAI pays more or whether Google’s work is more interesting. It is whether the OpenAI role would still satisfy your intrinsic needs: autonomy, mastery, meaning, relatedness, and growth.

If the answer is “no, it is mostly higher pay/title but worse daily work,” the research would lean toward staying with the more interesting job.

If the answer is “yes, it is less technically fascinating but gives me leadership, ownership, growth, and impact I genuinely want,” the research is much more favorable toward taking it.

answer:
There is not much research that directly answers: “Should an already well-paid AI researcher leave a more interesting job for a somewhat higher-paying, more managerial, less interesting one?” But there is a lot of adjacent evidence. The short version is:

> Across organizational psychology and well-being research, the content of the work — autonomy, intrinsic interest, skill use, meaning, person–job fit, learning, and social environment — tends to predict job satisfaction and broader well-being more strongly than marginal differences in pay, especially once income is already high.  
>  
> However, “leading a small team” may itself be an intrinsic/meaning/identity factor, not merely a status/pay factor. Regret research suggests people often regret not pursuing personally central aspirations, so if leadership is a genuine long-term aspiration, that matters.

Below are the main research findings I’d rely on.

---

## 1. “Interesting work” maps onto several well-studied predictors of job satisfaction

In the literature, “interesting work” is usually studied under labels like:

- intrinsic motivation  
- autonomy  
- skill variety  
- task identity  
- task significance  
- feedback  
- meaningful work  
- person–job fit  
- vocational interest congruence

The classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model** from Hackman & Oldham. Later meta-analyses found that jobs high in autonomy, variety, task significance, feedback, and identity are reliably associated with higher job satisfaction, internal motivation, and lower turnover intentions.

Useful references:

- Hackman & Oldham, 1976 — Job Characteristics Model  
- Fried & Ferris, 1987 — meta-analysis of the model  
- Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007 — large meta-analysis of work design

The Humphrey et al. meta-analysis is particularly relevant. Motivational job characteristics — autonomy, task variety, significance, feedback, etc. — had moderate-to-large relationships with job satisfaction and motivation. In social-science terms, these are not tiny effects.

So, if “interesting stuff” at Google means more autonomy, deeper technical challenge, more learning, better fit with your identity as a researcher/engineer, and more flow, the evidence says that is not a superficial preference. It is one of the core predictors of job satisfaction.

---

## 2. Person–job fit is one of the strongest predictors

A major meta-analysis by **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005** looked at person–job fit and person–organization fit. Person–job fit had a strong relationship with job satisfaction, organizational commitment, and lower intention to quit.

Roughly speaking, person–job fit tends to correlate with job satisfaction around the `.5` range in many studies, which is large for this kind of research.

That matters because your situation sounds less like:

> “One job pays more; one pays less.”

and more like:

> “One job fits my current intellectual interests better; the other fits my leadership aspirations better.”

The research would suggest you should compare the jobs on fit, not only on compensation/title.

---

## 3. Vocational interests predict satisfaction, performance, and persistence

There is also a long literature on **interest congruence**, often based on Holland’s RIASEC model. The basic result: people whose work fits their interests tend to be more satisfied, perform better, and stay longer.

References include:

- Spokane, 1985  
- Assouline & Meir, 1987  
- Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow, 2012

The effects are usually small-to-moderate, but robust. The important point is that “I find this work interesting” is not just a luxury. Interest–work fit predicts both subjective outcomes and career outcomes.

---

## 4. Pay matters, but marginal pay increases have weaker effects, especially at high income

The pay literature is nuanced.

A meta-analysis by **Judge et al., 2010** found that pay level is only modestly related to job satisfaction — much weaker than person–job fit or intrinsic work characteristics. Pay satisfaction matters, but absolute pay is not a huge predictor of job satisfaction once basic needs and fairness expectations are met.

On general well-being:

- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010** found that emotional well-being rose with income up to around $75k/year in 2010 USD, while life evaluation continued to rise with income.
- **Killingsworth, 2021** found experienced well-being continuing to rise with income beyond that, roughly log-linearly.
- **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023** reconciled some of the disagreement: for most people, higher income is associated with higher well-being, but with diminishing returns; for the least happy people, the relationship may flatten at higher incomes.

For your case, the key point is diminishing returns. If both jobs already pay very well, then “a bit higher salary” is unlikely, on average, to change day-to-day happiness as much as a large change in job content, autonomy, stress, or fit.

Exception: if the pay difference materially changes your life — financial independence timeline, family obligations, housing, immigration/security, ability to take future risks — then pay can matter a lot. But if it is mostly “nice to have” or status/scorekeeping, the research would usually weight it less.

---

## 5. Meaningful work is strongly related to well-being

“Interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical, but they often overlap. A meta-analysis by **Allan et al., 2019** found that meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction and moderately associated with life satisfaction and general well-being.

Meaningful work is often driven by:

- using valued skills  
- contributing to something important  
- autonomy  
- coherence with identity  
- positive social impact  
- belonging and recognition

So a key question is: which role is more meaningful to you? The Google role may be more intellectually meaningful; the OpenAI role may be more meaningful if leading people, mentoring, or organizational impact matters deeply to you.

The research does not say “always choose the most technically interesting work.” It says that meaning, autonomy, and fit are very important.

---

## 6. Promotions and management roles have mixed evidence

Moving into leadership can increase satisfaction for some people because it brings:

- more autonomy  
- more influence  
- higher status  
- broader impact  
- mentoring opportunities  
- career capital

But it can also bring:

- more meetings  
- less deep work  
- more conflict  
- responsibility without full control  
- stress from people-management  
- less hands-on technical contribution

The evidence on promotions is mixed but useful.

For example:

- **Kosteas, 2011** found that promotions and expectations of promotion are associated with higher job satisfaction.
- **Boyce & Oswald, 2012** found that job promotion did not necessarily improve health and was associated with increased mental strain in some data.

So “leading a small team” is not obviously good or bad. It depends on whether the actual daily work of leadership fits you. Wanting to lead is important, but it is worth distinguishing:

> “I want to do the day-to-day work of leading people”  
> from  
> “I want to have achieved the status/identity of being a leader.”

The first is more likely to produce lasting satisfaction than the second.

---

## 7. People often overestimate salient differences like salary/title and underestimate daily experience

Behavioral decision research suggests that when people compare options side-by-side, they overweight things that are easy to compare: salary, title, company prestige, scope, headcount. They may underweight things that are harder to quantify but dominate daily experience: boredom, meetings, intellectual energy, manager quality, autonomy.

Relevant ideas:

- **Hsee & Zhang, 2004** — distinction bias: side-by-side comparisons can overweight small quantitative differences.
- **Kahneman et al., 2006** — focusing illusion: “Nothing in life is as important as you think it is while you are thinking about it.”
- Affective forecasting research by Wilson & Gilbert: people often mispredict how much future events will affect their happiness.

In job decisions, this means salary/title can loom very large during the decision, while the actual calendar you live with every week may matter more afterward.

A useful research-consistent exercise is: compare the two jobs by expected weekly calendar, not by offer letter.

---

## 8. Regret research: people often regret inaction and abandoned aspirations

The regret literature is relevant but not decisive.

Findings:

- **Gilovich & Medvec, 1995**: in the short term, people often regret actions; in the long term, they more often regret inactions.
- **Roese & Summerville, 2005**: career and education are among the most common domains of regret.
- **Davidai & Gilovich, 2018**: people’s most enduring regrets often involve failures to pursue their “ideal self” — hopes, dreams, aspirations — more than failures of duty or obligation.

This cuts both ways in your case.

If “leading a team” is a long-standing ideal-self aspiration, you may later regret never trying it. But if doing frontier, intellectually exciting research is central to your ideal self, you may regret leaving that for a more managerial, less interesting path.

The research does not say: “People regret choosing interesting work.” Nor does it say: “People regret not taking the higher-status role.” It says people regret not pursuing central, identity-relevant aspirations, especially when the road not taken remains psychologically open.

---

## My research-based synthesis

If I compress the evidence into practical implications:

1. **Interesting, autonomous, meaningful work is a major predictor of job satisfaction.**  
   It is not a minor perk.

2. **At already-high income levels, a modest salary increase is usually a weaker predictor of happiness than work content and fit.**

3. **Leadership may be a strong positive if it satisfies intrinsic motives — mentoring, influence, building systems, developing people.**  
   But if it mainly brings status/pay while reducing the work you love, the evidence would be cautious.

4. **Regret depends on identity.**  
   If you have a deep aspiration to lead, not trying may become a regret. If your deeper aspiration is to do technically fascinating research, leaving may become the regret.

5. **The most evidence-aligned comparison is not Google vs OpenAI or salary vs interest.**  
   It is: which role gives you the better expected daily/weekly life across autonomy, learning, meaning, stress, social environment, and identity fit?

A compact decision question would be:

> Ignoring company prestige and compensation for a moment, which job’s actual weekly calendar would I rather live for the next two years?

And then separately:

> Is the leadership opportunity important enough to compensate for less interesting technical work — and is it something I want to actually do day-to-day, not just have on my CV?

Based on the research, if the salary difference is only modest and both jobs are already financially strong, I would give substantial weight to the more interesting work. The strongest counterweight is that leading a small team may be a genuine intrinsic/identity goal for you, not merely an extrinsic reward.

answer:
I don’t know of a clean study that exactly randomizes people between “more interesting work / lower pay” and “less interesting work / higher pay + leadership.” But there is a lot of adjacent evidence, and it is fairly consistent:

**Interesting, intrinsically motivating work is one of the stronger predictors of job satisfaction and well-being at work. Pay matters, but its marginal effect is usually much smaller—especially once income is already high. Leadership is a positive only if it gives you autonomy, mastery, impact, and identity-congruent growth; otherwise people often adapt to the title/pay and are left with the day-to-day work.**

Some relevant research results:

| Research area | Main finding | Relevance to your case |
|---|---|---|
| **Job characteristics research** — Hackman & Oldham; Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007 meta-analysis | Jobs with more autonomy, skill variety, task significance, feedback, and task identity are reliably associated with higher job satisfaction, internal motivation, and lower turnover intentions. Correlations with satisfaction are often moderate-to-large by psych standards. | “Interesting work” often bundles several of these: novelty, autonomy, complexity, use of skill, meaningful progress. Losing that can matter a lot. |
| **Person–job fit / interest fit** — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005 meta-analysis; vocational interest congruence studies | Fit between the person and the job is strongly related to job satisfaction and commitment, and negatively related to intent to quit. Interest congruence has smaller but reliable effects on satisfaction and performance. | If your current work is unusually well-matched to what you naturally find fascinating, that is not a minor perk; it is a major predictor of long-term satisfaction. |
| **Intrinsic motivation and creativity** — Amabile; Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford 2014 meta-analysis | Intrinsic motivation predicts performance, especially quality/creative performance. External incentives are more predictive for quantity or simpler tasks. | For research/engineering work, being genuinely interested in the problem likely affects both happiness and output. |
| **Pay and job satisfaction** — Judge et al. 2010 meta-analysis | Pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction; reported correlations are around the small range, e.g. roughly \( r \approx .15 \). Pay satisfaction matters more than absolute pay, but even there it is not usually the dominant driver. | A “somewhat higher salary” may improve satisfaction, but usually less than people expect, particularly when both options already pay extremely well. |
| **Income and subjective well-being** — Kahneman & Deaton 2010; Killingsworth 2021; Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers 2023 | Income is related to well-being, but mostly logarithmically/diminishingly. Newer work suggests well-being can keep rising at high incomes, but the effect is still much smaller than the difference between miserable vs engaging daily work. | At Google/OpenAI compensation levels, marginal salary probably matters mainly if it changes your life options: financial independence, family security, risk tolerance, location, etc. |
| **Self-determination theory** — Deci & Ryan; Van den Broeck et al. | Well-being at work is strongly tied to autonomy, competence/mastery, and relatedness. Goals aligned with intrinsic values predict better well-being than purely extrinsic goals. | The question is not simply “interesting vs salary”; it is whether the OpenAI role satisfies autonomy, competence, relatedness, and growth better or worse than your current one. |
| **Promotions / job changes** — “honeymoon–hangover” literature, e.g. Boswell et al. | Job changes and promotions often produce an initial satisfaction boost, then partial adaptation. The durable part depends on whether the new role actually improves day-to-day work characteristics. | “Leading a small team” may feel exciting initially. The key is whether you will enjoy the actual weekly calendar of that role after the novelty fades. |
| **Regret research** — Gilovich & Medvec; Roese & Summerville | Long-term regrets are often about inaction or missed opportunities, and career/education are common domains of regret. But the research does not say “always take the offer”; it says people regret unrealized possibilities that remain psychologically open. | You might regret not trying leadership; you might also regret leaving unusually fascinating work for money/status. The relevant question is which foregone path will feel like the bigger “unlived life.” |

A useful way to translate the research into your decision:

### 1. Separate “interesting work” into components

Ask which of these your current job has and the OpenAI job would have:

- **Autonomy:** Will you control what you work on?
- **Mastery:** Will you keep getting better at something you care about?
- **Problem fascination:** Do the core technical problems pull you in?
- **Impact/significance:** Does the work matter to you?
- **Feedback loops:** Will you see progress and learn quickly?
- **Social fit:** Will you like/respect the people?
- **Identity fit:** Does the role feel like “the kind of work I want my life to contain”?

The literature suggests these are more predictive than title or salary alone.

### 2. Treat leadership as its own intrinsic-interest variable

You said leading a small team is something you have always wanted. That matters. The research would not classify that as merely “extrinsic” if you genuinely want the craft of leadership: mentoring, setting direction, building a team, making others effective.

But management can also mean less deep technical work, more coordination, ambiguity, conflict, recruiting, performance management, and meetings. So the question is:

> Do I want the daily practice of leading, or do I want the status/option value of having led?

If it is the former, the OpenAI role may be more interesting in a different dimension. If it is the latter, the evidence says the satisfaction gain may fade.

### 3. Be skeptical of the salary difference unless it changes real constraints

The pay literature does not say money is irrelevant. It matters a lot when it buys security, freedom, location choice, reduced stress, or earlier financial independence. But if both jobs already put you in a very high-income bracket, then a moderate salary increase is less likely to compensate for a large drop in day-to-day interest.

A useful test:

> Would I still seriously consider the OpenAI role if the salary were the same?

If yes, leadership/growth may be the real draw.  
If no, the evidence would make me cautious, because pay alone is a relatively weak predictor of sustained job satisfaction.

### 4. The strongest evidence-based warning

People often overestimate the durable happiness from external changes—salary, prestige, title—and underestimate the importance of repeated daily experience. If the OpenAI job’s average Tuesday looks substantially less engaging, that is a real cost.

### 5. The strongest evidence-based argument for moving

If the OpenAI role gives you:

- more autonomy,
- a leadership challenge you intrinsically want,
- a steep learning curve,
- strong colleagues,
- broader impact,
- and better future optionality,

then even if the technical topic is less fascinating, it may still score higher on the job-characteristics dimensions that predict satisfaction.

So the research does **not** simply say “choose the more interesting technical work.” It says:

> Choose the role with the better long-term fit to your intrinsic motivations, autonomy, mastery, relationships, and sense of purpose. Do not overweight marginal salary or title unless they materially change your life.

In your specific framing, the crux is whether “leading a small team” is itself deeply interesting to you, or whether it is mainly attractive as advancement. If it is deeply interesting, the OpenAI offer may be more aligned than your description first suggests. If the OpenAI work would be genuinely dull and the appeal is mostly compensation/title, the research would lean toward staying with the more interesting work or trying to get leadership where you are.
